title: Exact relations between the density-density correlators of states in a spin multiplet
abstract: We present exact identities relating the pair-correlation functions and static structure factors of states in a spin multiplet. This allows us to compute these density-density correlation functions of all members of the multiplet using just these correlation functions of the highest-weight state. We apply these relations to obtain energies for many fractional quantum Hall (FQH) states. In particular, we analytically compute the energies of the Halperin-$(1,1,1)$ state as a function of density imbalance and layer separation, and numerically evaluate these energies for many other FQH states.
summary: This paper provides a new analytical method to significantly reduce the computational complexity of studying spin-degenerate many-body systems. By deriving exact relations between states in a spin multiplet, the authors show that one only needs to calculate the correlation functions for a single fully polarized state to know the properties of the entire multiplet. They demonstrate the utility of this method by analytically computing the energy of the Halperin-(1,1,1) state and numerically evaluating various other fractional quantum Hall states. This approach is particularly useful for studying bilayer and moiré systems where spin or pseudospin degrees of freedom are present.
main_problem: Computing density-density correlation functions and energies for all members of a spin multiplet is computationally expensive because each state must typically be evaluated separately.
main_result: The authors derive exact identities that allow the pair-correlation functions and static structure factors of any state in a spin multiplet to be computed using only the properties of the highest-weight (fully polarized) state.
method: The authors use SU(2) spin rotation symmetry to derive analytical relations between correlation functions and apply these to compute thermodynamic energies via integration of the structure factors.
model_or_system: The study focuses on many-body systems with spin or pseudospin (layer, orbital, or valley) degrees of freedom, specifically fractional quantum Hall (FQH) states in bilayer or multi-component systems.
key_observables: Pair-correlation function g(r), static structure factor S(q), and ground-state Coulomb energy E0.
important_parameters: Layer separation (d/l), polarization/density imbalance (gamma), and total spin quantum number (S).
main_assumptions: The relations are limited to states within the same spin multiplet and rely on the assumption of SU(2) spin-rotation symmetry; energy calculations rely on approximate functional forms for g(r).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows density plots of Coulomb energies for various FQH states (such as Halperin-(1,1,1) and Moore-Read) as a function of layer separation and polarization.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the computational problem, derives exact analytical identities for correlation functions using SU(2) symmetry, applies these relations to calculate energies for specific FQH states, and discusses results for bilayer systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Coulomb energies in units of e2/(εℓ) in the thermodynamic limit of many fully polarized, i.e., with maximal total spin, fractional quantum Hall states as a function of layer separation, d/ℓ, and polarization, γ, which labels the z−component of the spin. Here, ℓis the magnetic length and ε is the dielectric constant of the background host material.
